caveats: a: No six-digit occupational mix or compensation-weighted task study was found; federal agency evidence is not representative of governors' or local executive offices.
a: The frozen dataset provides no receipts denominator, so labor opportunity cannot be linked to a conventional private-firm labor share.
rho: Federal AI governance and capacity differ materially from state and local governments.
rho: Time saved may improve briefing quality or responsiveness without reducing payroll or avoiding hires.
e: Government contractors serving executive offices belong to other NAICS codes and cannot be imported into this record.
e: Treating public offices as firms would create a category error rather than a conservative estimate.
s5: A change in elected or appointed officeholder does not transfer ownership of the government establishment.
s5: Privatization of selected support functions would create contractors in other industry codes, not a sale of the executive office.
q: Budget savings may be reappropriated, absorbed by new mandates, or converted into service quality rather than retained as operating profit.
q: Procurement savings accruing to an office are different from commercial pricing power.
d5: Overall public employment is not a measure of demand for executive-office services and is dominated by functions outside this code.
d5: Government demand is mandate- and budget-driven rather than a market quantity, so constant-quality volume is inherently difficult to observe.
o: Supporting work can move to shared-service agencies or contractors even when executive authority remains internal.
o: The primitive's operator concept was designed for private firms and maps imperfectly to a public institution.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: These offices exercise governmental executive authority and provide policy direction, coordination, representation, appointments, budgeting, communications, and constituent-facing administration funded primarily by appropriations and taxes rather than recurring fees from external customers.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default LMM outsourced-service firm lens is structurally inapplicable: Census defines this code exclusively as government establishments, the frozen dataset has no receipts concept, and no defensible firm count exists. The packet preserves the full government-office population to assess work exposure and service durability, while marking firm-transfer and commercial-retention constructs missing rather than inventing a private-company subset.
lens.excluded_activities: Private management consulting and administrative-services firms, campaign organizations, legislative bodies, combined executive-legislative bodies, courts, public-finance agencies, operating departments, captive units classified elsewhere, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. federal, state, county, municipal, and town government establishments serving as offices of chief executives, including presidents, governors, mayors, city and town managers, county executives or supervisors, and their executive advisory committees and commissions.

a.bridge: BLS describes executive administrative work as research, report preparation, document review, scheduling, correspondence, database maintenance, and meeting support, while top executives analyze reports, budgets, performance, policy, contracts, and organizational operations. GAO documents extensive current and planned federal AI use. I translate these cross-industry occupations and federal-agency use cases to executive-office work, weighting substantial document, research, communications, scheduling, and coordination opportunity against irreducibly human political judgment, negotiation, representation, appointments, crisis leadership, and democratic accountability. The proxy may overstate exposure in small offices where staff roles are highly interpersonal and understate it in large offices with dedicated policy and communications teams.
caveats: No six-digit occupational mix or compensation-weighted task study was found; federal agency evidence is not representative of governors' or local executive offices.
The frozen dataset provides no receipts denominator, so labor opportunity cannot be linked to a conventional private-firm labor share.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory for federal, state, and local executive offices by occupation and office size, including current AI use, task hours, sensitivity level, and human-review requirements, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can draft and summarize briefings, compare policies, search records, triage correspondence, prepare meeting materials, monitor commitments, translate communications, organize schedules, and support budget or scenario analysis. Final policy choices, public representation, coalition building, negotiation, personnel decisions, crisis command, privileged advice, and exercise of lawful authority remain human responsibilities.

d5.bridge: Census reports total state and local government employment of 20.3 million in March 2025, 1.4% above 2024, but that broad population includes education, hospitals, police, and many functions outside executive offices. I use broad public-employment stability and growth as a weak demand anchor, then compress the five-year range because each jurisdiction continues to require executive leadership while office staffing changes with population, budgets, consolidation, mandates, and administration preferences. The proxy likely overstates executive-office growth when expansion is concentrated in operating services and understates it when coordination burdens rise faster than total employment.
caveats: Overall public employment is not a measure of demand for executive-office services and is dominated by functions outside this code.
Government demand is mandate- and budget-driven rather than a market quantity, so constant-quality volume is inherently difficult to observe.
d5.change_evidence: A consistent federal, state, and local time series for NAICS 921110 establishments, staffing, inflation-adjusted appropriations, workload indicators, jurisdiction formation or consolidation, and outsourced support would materially improve the estimate.
d5.rationale: Population, emergency coordination, intergovernmental programs, public communications, data governance, and policy complexity support stable or modestly increasing work. Fiscal stress, shared services, municipal consolidation, digitization, flatter staffs, and changing political priorities constrain headcount and service volume.

caveats: Government contractors serving executive offices belong to other NAICS codes and cannot be imported into this record.
Treating public offices as firms would create a category error rather than a conservative estimate.
e.change_evidence: A formally approved alternative methodology for public-sector institutions, with a defined denominator and eligibility construct that replaces EBITDA and private control, would be required before this primitive could be estimated.
e.rationale: There is no defensible share of LMM firms eligible under the default lens because Census defines the industry as government establishments, the frozen dataset provides no LMM firm count, and appropriations-funded public offices do not have normalized EBITDA or transferable private control.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($13,619,209,546) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Supporting work can move to shared-service agencies or contractors even when executive authority remains internal.
The primitive's operator concept was designed for private firms and maps imperfectly to a public institution.
o.change_evidence: Statutory or constitutional changes allowing autonomous systems or external vendors to exercise executive authority, combined with observed elimination of executive-office institutions at scale, would lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Executive authority, appointments, public representation, lawful approvals, crisis command, political judgment, and accountability continue to require a recognized government office and human officeholder. AI and contractors can supply analysis, drafting, communications, and administrative support, and some back-office tasks may be shared or outsourced, but software cannot become the accountable constitutional or statutory executive within five years.

caveats: Budget savings may be reappropriated, absorbed by new mandates, or converted into service quality rather than retained as operating profit.
Procurement savings accruing to an office are different from commercial pricing power.
q.change_evidence: A methodology that replaces commercial retention with a public-value or budget-retention construct, specifies treatment of appropriations and service-quality gains, and supplies auditable office-level cost data would be required.
q.rationale: Executive offices are funded by appropriations and taxes rather than customer prices, renewals, or commercial fee contracts. The share of an implemented gross benefit retained after customer pass-through and price competition therefore has no defensible denominator or observed mechanism under this code.

caveats: Federal AI governance and capacity differ materially from state and local governments.
Time saved may improve briefing quality or responsiveness without reducing payroll or avoiding hires.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments across a representative sample of federal, gubernatorial, county, and municipal executive offices reporting usage, error and override rates, security accreditation, staff hours saved, procurement time, and sustained adoption after leadership transitions would narrow the estimate.
rho.rationale: Executive offices can deploy secure search, drafting, correspondence, scheduling, translation, and analytic tools within five years, and federal agencies already report many AI use cases. Implementation is constrained by procurement cycles, fragmented state and local capacity, legacy records, cybersecurity, privacy, public-records law, records retention, accessibility, bias, political sensitivity, model errors, changing administrations, and the need for accountable human review. GAO found incomplete or inaccurate use-case inventories and a large body of governance requirements, signaling material operational friction.

caveats: A change in elected or appointed officeholder does not transfer ownership of the government establishment.
Privatization of selected support functions would create contractors in other industry codes, not a sale of the executive office.
s5.change_evidence: A public-sector replacement construct explicitly defining which institutional changes qualify and how they map to the screen would be necessary; ordinary M&A or owner-succession data cannot answer this question.
s5.rationale: Elections, appointments, successions, reorganizations, and changes of administration do not constitute qualifying transfers of control in an eligible private operating company. With no eligible-firm denominator, a five-year control-transfer probability is undefined.

principal_driver: Document-heavy policy coordination and public communication create broad assistive AI opportunity across executive-office staffs.
principal_weakness: The code contains no private LMM firms, transferable ownership, receipts denominator, or commercial benefit-retention mechanism.
